title: Investigation Classification Standard
purpose: Defines how agents classify discovered UX into the UX system hierarchy so route observations become durable specs instead of loose notes.

classificationTargets.useWhen: A broad product area or route family needs a high-level behavior owner.
A smaller coherent UX surface has its own user goals, flows, validation, or proof needs.
Behavior has a clear UX owner even if the codebase does not have a matching physical submodule.
A user goal crosses one or more flows or requires context, motivation, success, hesitation, or recovery modeling.
A coherent path has entry, happy path, alternates, blocked states, and exit behavior.
A reusable or proof-worthy part of a flow needs its own requirements.
A meaningful user-visible behavior needs to be specified as current, desired, proposed, or pending.
A runtime/user-visible situation changes behavior, options, recovery, or proof expectations.
A behavior must be proven through manual or automated validation.
A missing product, behavior, ownership, or proof decision cannot be safely derived.
A branch must be tracked as covered, missing, pending, omitted, out of scope, needs confirmation, or needs proof.

rules: Prefer the smallest coherent UX owner that makes behavior discoverable.
Use implied subfeatures when UX ownership is real but code folders do not reflect it.
Do not create duplicate governing rows for the same behavior concept.
A route can contain multiple features or subfeatures; a feature can span multiple routes.
When classification is unclear, create a pending ownership/classification decision rather than hiding ambiguity.
